Tafseer:
The eloquence of the word 'Takwir':
'Takwir' means to wrap something up or bind it in the form of a bundle. 'Kawwara-l-imamata 'ala ra'sihi' means: 'he wrapped the turban over his head'.
The state of the sun at the occurrence of the Resurrection: This portrays the state of the sun—which is the most prominent and magnificent object of the heavens, indeed of this entire universe—at the occurrence of the Resurrection, showing that it will be wrapped up. Obviously, when the sun itself is wrapped up, this entire world, which is illuminated by its brightness, will become dark. Although we witness the setting of the sun daily, its nature is completely different: that is merely because we are veiled from it; but when the upheaval of the Resurrection occurs, the entire system of the sun will be devastated. Who can estimate the darkness when the sun itself loses its light!

The state of the stars:
'Inkidar' means becoming dim, faded, and falling. Here, it refers to the stars losing their light. Obviously, when the sun itself is wrapped up, all the lamps and lights associated with its system will lose their light on their own.

The state of the mountains:
After the heaven, this points to the most magnificent and grand object of the earth—the mountains—showing that these mountains fixed in the earth, which you assume are permanent, stable, and immovable, (Footnote 1) will be removed and set in motion on that Day. Only their being set in motion is mentioned here, but elsewhere it is explained that they will fly about like clouds.
_____
Footnote 1: It should noted that when the deniers of the Resurrection were warned of the upheaval of the Resurrection, they would mock and ask: 'When the Resurrection comes, will it also uproot the mountains?'

The neglect of beloved possessions:
'Ishar' is the plural of 'ushara''. This word refers to a she-camel that has been pregnant for ten months—namely, close to giving birth.
After describing the transience of grand objects, this explains the worthlessness of beloved possessions: the upheaval of that Day will cause such a state of distress that no one will care for even their most beloved wealth.
This is stated keeping in view the specific taste of the primary addressees of the Qur'an—the Arabs. Among their possessions, the most valued status belonged to their camels. Particularly, those she-camels were highly beloved to them that had been pregnant for ten months, and whose time of giving birth was close. The owners of such she-camels naturally took special care of them, as many of their future hopes depended on them. Referencing these beloved she-camels as an example, the heedless, who are trapped in the love of the world, are reminded of the terror of the Hereafter: its very first phase will be so severe that no one will care for even their most beloved possessions. The pregnant she-camels will wander neglected, but their owners will be so occupied with their own state that they will have no concern for anything else, no matter how beloved. This same is explained elsewhere as:
(On the Day every nursing mother will forget her nursling) (Al-Hajj, 22:2).
The only difference is that here, the terror of that Day is explained through the neglect of beloved wealth, whereas there, it is explained through the maternal affection becoming numb—even though this affection is so strong that no worldly calamity can normally suppress it.

The state of the wild beasts:
That is: not only humans, but even the wild beasts will be in such a state of distress due to the terror of that Day that they will gather together at places where they expect refuge, forgetting their natural enmities. When a forest fire occurs or floodwaters spread, wild beasts gather in panic at a high ground where they expect safety, and the terror of the shared calamity is such that sheep, lions, and wolves stand side-by-side, but no one is aware that his rival or prey is next to him. This very state will occur in its most terrifying form at the occurrence of the Resurrection. The subsequent verse:
(And when the seas are erupted) (verse 6)
shows that the seas will overflow their boundaries and spread over the earth, causing this universal calamity that will strike even the wild beasts with distress.

The eruption of the seas:
'Tasjeer' literally means filling an oven with fuel and lighting it, and then metaphorically extended to the eruption of rivers and seas. When rivers overflow their banks and spread over the earth, this word is used. To express this, the word 'tafjeer' (gushing forth) is also used, and in the subsequent Surah, which is its twin, this is expressed as: (And when the seas are erupted [fujjirat]) (Al-Infitar, 82:3). The meaning is: while today these seas are restricted within their boundaries, when the upheaval of the Resurrection occurs, they will erupt and spread over the entire surface.

The conditions after the occurrence of the Resurrection:
While the preceding verses described the conditions at the occurrence of the Resurrection, this and the subsequent verses describe the conditions after its occurrence.
It is stated: when the souls are paired. Pairing means classifying people into different groups based on their beliefs and deeds. This points to the reality detailed in Surah Al-Waqi'ah, verses 7-44:
(And you become three kinds)
explaining that while in this world, the righteous and the wicked live together, this state will not remain forever; rather, a Day is bound to come when people will be classified based on their faith and deeds. On that Day, those who are successful in the scale of justice will receive eternal success and kingdom, and those who lived heedless of it will face eternal loss and disgrace. This points to their division into three major groups—the foremost (sabiqun), the companions of the right (as-hab-ul-yameen), and the companions of the left (as-hab-ush-shimal)—describing the treatment each group will receive. This is intended to warn people: this world is for trial and examination, where both good and bad are granted respite; but in the new world after the Resurrection, the worshippers of falsehood will be cast into Hell, and those who succeeded in the trial will receive eternal honor.

Redress of the innocent and oppressed:
This points to the justice of that Day of Justice, and presents the example of the buried-alive girl to show that first of all, those innocent oppressed who fell victim to the tyranny of those appointed as their protectors will receive redress. 'Maw'udah' refers to a girl buried alive. Some uncivilized Arab tribes of Jahiliyyah buried their daughters alive, mostly out of fear of poverty, and occasionally due to excessive pride. Since the fathers themselves buried these girls and possessed absolute authority over them, there was no question of their seeking redress in this world.
The temperament of the court of the Hereafter: Pointing to the redress of these silent oppressed, the Qur'an clarifies the temperament of the court of the Hereafter—showing that first of all, those who were most helpless and weak in this world, unable to seek redress for the tyranny they faced, will receive justice. They will be called first and asked: 'For what sin were you killed?' This is a highly severe warning to those hard-hearted fathers, showing that if these silent and innocent girls could not seek redress in the world, they should not assume there is no one to ask about them with God. On that Day, Allah Himself will prosecute their murder. He will ask the oppressed: 'For what sin were you killed?' The purpose of this question, obviously, is for the Lord of Majesty Himself to bring the case of their murder—which could not go to any court in this world—to His own court and decide it.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 8 and 9 was combined in the original text.]

Everyone's record before them:
'Suhuf' refers to people's records of deeds, and their being opened means everyone's record will be laid before them, as stated further on:
(A soul will know what it has brought [i.e., done]) (verse 14).

The heaven will turn red:
'Kasht' literally means removing the outer layer that covers something, such as skinning a slaughtered animal. This is a well-known word in Arabic, and after skinning, the flesh appears red. This metaphorically describes the heaven turning red. In Surah Ar-Rahman, the words: (And becomes rose-colored like oil) (37) appeared, and here, the subsequent verse mentions the burning of Hell, which is a clear context showing that this redness of the heaven will be due to the burning of Hell.

'Tas'eer' means burning and heating. While Hell is already prepared, when the time comes to cast the criminals into it, it will be specifically heated to burn them, and when the criminals are cast in, it will burn with greater intensity.

Paradise brought near for the righteous:
'Izlaf' means bringing near. That is: it will be brought near for those righteous who deserve it. This reveals that Paradise is also prepared beforehand, but its veil will be lifted when people are classified. In Surah Qaf, it is clarified that bringing it near does not mean it will be brought from a distance; it will not be distant, but close, yet it will be brought even closer as an offering to honor and welcome the people of Paradise, as stated:
(And Paradise will be brought near to the righteous, not far) (Qaf, 50:31).

Answering all the conditional clauses:
This is the collective answer to all the 'idha' (when) clauses mentioned above. That is: when these conditions occur, then every soul will know what it has brought before its Lord. Knowing, obviously, means knowing its end. The meaning is: those who are mocking it today may mock, but they should remember that this Day is coming with a great upheaval, and on that Day, everyone will see what preparation they made and what they ignored. In the subsequent twin Surah, this is expressed as:
(A soul will know what it has sent before and kept back) (Al-Infitar, 82:5).

A fabrication to deny the Qur'an:
This refutes the delusion the deniers fabricated regarding Allah's Messenger and His Book to turn their common people away from the Qur'an, preventing them from being awed by its divine nature. When they saw that people were being affected by the Prophet's call and the warning of the Resurrection was becoming a reality, which could ultimately lead to their supporting this call, they fabricated this accusation: 'This Qur'an is not a divine revelation, nor is its presenter a messenger of Allah; rather, he is like our soothsayers, and just as soothsayers are in contact with the jinn who cast news upon them, similarly he is in contact with some devil who casts words upon him, which he presents as a divine revelation, claiming to be a messenger of Allah.'
The Qur'an has refuted this accusation in various places, particularly in Surah Ash-Shu'ara and Surah An-Najm, and we have discussed all its aspects. Here, too, this accusation is refuted from a new perspective, to understand which it is necessary to know the basis of the soothsaying common among the Arabs.
Soothsaying was based on two things:
First, their assumed astrology. They believed the stars are self-operating entities, claiming some are auspicious and some are inauspicious. Similarly, they fabricated various delusions regarding their rising, setting, falling, and movements, based on which they made various predictions to deceive their common people and claim knowledge of the unseen. For example, they would spread the rumor: 'Due to the retrograde movement of a certain star, a major danger is approaching; so people should consult us to know the remedies.' Those who fell into their trap were thoroughly deceived.
Second, they claimed to be in contact with the jinn to learn the secrets of the unseen. We explained in Surah Ash-Shu'ara that when someone sought their guidance, they would pretend to meditate and then present a rhymed, rhythmic speech—often meaningless—claiming it was revealed by a jinni who knew the secrets of the unseen.
Dealing a blow to the pillars of soothsaying: The Qur'an has dealt a blow to both of these pillars of soothsaying. It presented the rising, setting, and movements of the sun, moon, and stars in a manner showing they are not self-operating, auspicious, or inauspicious entities, but are completely in the hand of the Creator of the universe. When He wills, He makes them rise, and when He wills, He makes them set. They bear witness that they are subservient to the Lord of the heavens and earth:
'Life brought me, and death took me away;
I came not by my own choice, nor do I leave by my choice.'
Reflect on the progressive development of Abraham's call in Surah Al-An'am, verses 75-84. It is sufficient to clarify the Qur'an's method of arguing and its logical conclusion.
Similarly, to deal a blow to the second pillar, the Qur'an referred to the shooting stars, clarifying that no jinni has any access to the high assembly. If they try to overhear, Allah has arranged in the stars that shooting stars are pelted at them from their towers. This is mentioned in various places, but to understand these oaths, refer to the related verses of Surah An-Najm and Surah Al-Jinn.
In the light of this introduction, reflect on the words and their meanings:
Analysis of the oaths: 'So I swear by the retreating [stars]' (fa-la uqsimu bil-khunnas) - We have explained that oaths in the Qur'an are sworn as testimonies for a claim, and the use of 'la' before the oath is to refute the listener's assumption.
'Khunnas' is the plural of 'khanis', which means those that retreat, disappear after appearing, or become hidden after becoming visible. This word is used as an attribute of the stars, and is so well known for them that mentioning the noun itself is not necessary. Some have restricted it to certain specific stars, but there is no proof for this. The attributes of the stars mentioned here apply to all of them—whether fixed stars or planets, Saturn, Mercury, or others.

'Those that run [their courses] and disappear' (al-jawari-l-kunnas) - This describes the further attributes of the stars, and their appearing without a conjunction proves that they share the same noun.
'Jawari' means those that run, and 'kunnas' is the plural of 'kanis'. 'Kanasa-dh-dhaby' means: the deer hid in its maman (refuge). 'Kanasati-n-nujum' means: the stars ran their courses and then disappeared in their resting places. The author of Aqrab al-Mawarid clarified that this is a shared attribute of all stars.
The oath by the stars in the context of nullifying soothsaying: The oath by the stars is sworn here to nullify soothsaying. We pointed out that the soothsayers' assumed knowledge of the unseen was based on two things: first, regarding the stars as self-operating entities; second, the delusion that there are stations in the heaven where the jinn sit to overhear the secrets of the unseen. Referencing these attributes of the stars, the Qur'an nullified both of these false beliefs. Their rising, setting, and movements in a highly disciplined system—without a single second's difference—bear clear witness that they are not self-operating entities, but are subservient to a Wise and All-Powerful Creator. Therefore, He alone is the Lord and Protector, not these subservient stars.
To nullify the second delusion, the Qur'an revealed a secret of the universe: these stars do not serve as stations for the jinn to overhear, but contain towers and guards from where shooting stars are pelted at the devils who try to overhear.
These attributes of the stars are described in the form of oaths; therefore, they are highly brief. Yet, the words contain clear indications for those who reflect—for example, 'khunnas' points to their setting, showing those who worshipped their rising that they should also look at their setting, which is a proof of their being subservient.
Similarly, 'al-jawari-l-kunnas' contains a subtle indication to the movement these stars make in pursuing the devils. When they try to overhear, a shooting star is seen to rush like an arrow, and then disappear after reaching its target.

This is the second oath, serving as another testimony to the claim.
'As'as' has been interpreted as becoming dark, and also as departing. Although both meanings are correct, I prefer the second meaning. This is because this same oath appeared in Surah Al-Muzzammil, verses 33-34, where the word 'adbar' (departed) was used instead of 'as'as':
(And by the night when it departs, and by the morning when it brightens).
There too, this oath was followed by proving the truth of the Qur'an's warning, and here too, it is followed by confirming the same claim.

This is the second oath, serving as another testimony to the claim.
'And by the morning when it breathes' (wa-s-subhi idha tanaffas) - 'tanaffas' means appearing, but this style carries a specific eloquence: it depicts the morning as if it was suppressed under the heavy load of the night, and then Allah Almighty removed this heavy load, allowing it to breathe and raise its head.

The primary claim:
This is the subject of the oaths, serving as the primary claim. It is stated: indeed, this Qur'an is the word of a noble messenger. 'Messenger' (rasul) here refers to Gabriel. The subsequent attributes clarify that they refer to Gabriel.
This same is stated from the negative aspect in verse 25: (And it is not the word of a expelled devil) (wa ma huwa bi-qawli shaytanir-rajeem). The meaning is: unfortunate are those who regard this Qur'an as the بکواس (nonsense) of soothsayers and call the Prophet a soothsayer. What soothsayers present is the casting of devils, in which there is no trace of truth. They claim to know the unseen, but their devils have no access to the high assembly and are pelted with shooting stars when they try to overhear. In contrast, this speech is brought by a messenger of Allah who is highly مقرب (near) and honored in His presence.

The further attributes of Gabriel:
These describe the further attributes of Gabriel to show the secure and pure source of the Qur'an, in contrast to the source from which soothsayers obtain their knowledge. It is stated: this noble messenger is possessed of great power. Allah Almighty has blessed him with such high capabilities that no other force can overcome, influence, or prevent him from executing his duties, nor can they alter or snatch anything from him. He executes the commands of the Owner of the Throne with complete authority because he holds a highly مقرب (secure) position in His presence. He has direct access to Him, and no one is between them. This attribute of Gabriel appeared in Surah An-Najm, verses 5-6, as: (taught him by one mighty in power, endued with wisdom).

'Obeyed' (muta') - meaning the angels under his command obey him without hesitation; they cannot deviate from his command, nor alter His message, nor collude with the devils.
The analysis of 'thamma': 'thamma ameen' (trustworthy there) - 'thamma' is used to point to a specific place, and also to emphasize an attribute. For example, in Surah Ash-Shu'ara: (And We brought near there [thamma] the others) (verse 64), which means: We brought the Egyptians to the very path from which We saved the Children of Israel, so that they drowned.
Similarly, in Surah Ad-Dahr: (And when you look there [thamma], you will see blessings and a great kingdom) (verse 20). The meaning is: wherever you look, you will see a great blessing and a magnificent kingdom.
Similarly, when used before an attribute, it emphasizes its grandeur. Here, appearing before 'ameen' (trustworthy), it draws attention to this specific quality of Gabriel: along with his other qualities, his most important quality is that he is highly trustworthy.
This is emphasized because this quality is the guarantee that whatever he brings from Allah contains no mixture or alteration. In contrast, the devils from whom soothsayers obtain their knowledge are thieves. They have no access to the high assembly, and if they try to overhear, they receive a lie, to which they add further lies before casting it upon their soothsayers, who are even greater liars. The meaning is: what comparison can there be between that dirty water and this pure spring from which the Prophet is fed!

Warning to the Quraysh:
Having clarified the grandeur of the source of the Qur'an, this addresses the leaders of the Quraysh: if your companion (Muhammad) claims that Allah has appointed him to warn you, and what he is reciting is His word revealed through His most مقرب (near) angel, do not regard his words as madness; it is a reality.
Using the word 'sahibukum' (your companion) is highly eloquent. The meaning is: he is not a stranger to you. He was born among you, lived with you, and every phase of his life passed before your eyes, and everyone among you has been a witness to his nobility, composure, truthfulness, and trustworthiness. Now, if his warning is heavy on you, reflect on his words keeping his character in view, instead of calling him mad, a soothsayer, or a poet.

That is: if he claims to have seen the angel who brings the revelation, this is also a reality, not a delusion. He saw him on the clear horizon. 'The clear horizon' (al-ufuq-ul-mubeen) refers to the part of the sky that is before the sight, where observation is completely clear. In Surah An-Najm, this is described as 'the high horizon':
(taught him by one mighty in power, endued with wisdom. So he stood poised, while he was on the high horizon. Then he approached and let himself down, until he was two-bows' length off or even closer. And He revealed to His servant what He revealed. The heart lied not in what he saw. Will you then dispute with him as to what he saw?) (An-Najm, 53:5-12).
Reflect on these verses. They refer to the visions the Prophet had at the beginning of his mission. When he mentioned them to his people, they rose to oppose him, calling it a delusion. Addressing them, it is stated: it is neither a delusion nor a trick, but a clear vision on the horizon, in which there is no room for doubt.

The Messenger is not miserly with the unseen:
That is: our Messenger is not greedy regarding the unseen like your soothsayers, who claim to know the unseen based on whatever delusion crosses their minds; rather, the vision he has and the revelation he receives are from Allah, which he presents to you. Your soothsayers rely on lies to maintain their business, but you know well that your companion has never chased such things. What he presents is not a display of knowing the unseen to gather wealth, but a reality he is bound to communicate.
'Dhaneen' is generally translated as 'miserly', but I have translated it as 'greedy'. Miserliness and greed are interconnected; hence, the difference is minor. I preferred 'greedy' because when 'dhann' means miserly, it takes the preposition 'bi', whereas here it takes 'ala', which indicates it implies 'greed'.

What was stated in: 'Indeed, it is the word of a noble messenger' is repeated here in a negative style to confirm: it is not the word of an expelled devil.
'Rajeem' (expelled/stoned) is the contrast to 'kareem' (noble). The meaning is: the angel who brings the revelation is highly noble, whereas the devils who descend on your soothsayers are expelled. 'Rajeem' means stoned; we explained that when they try to overhear, they are stoned with shooting stars; hence, this is their permanent attribute.

This expresses amazement at their stubbornness: 'Where are you going, turning away from this Qur'an? What comparison can there be between it and the nonsense of your soothsayers!' The meaning is: in your stubbornness, you have lost the capacity to distinguish between precious gems and worthless stones.

Finally, this is a highly effective warning: this is a reminder for the worlds before that Day arrives, which is bound to happen and from which there is no escape. If you accept it, you will benefit yourselves, and accepting or rejecting it is your own responsibility, not the responsibility of Allah or the Messenger. So whoever wishes may choose the straight path; otherwise, let them be prepared to face the end of which they are warned.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 27 and 28 was combined in the original text.]

This points to the divine law regarding the guidance to faith—showing that He blesses with guidance only those who seek it and use their capabilities for it. Those who choose to remain blind and deaf are left to wander in their deviation. This law has appeared in various places, including under the final verses of Surah Al-Muzzammil.
